I'll search for this paper to verify the exact title and author information.
The search results clearly confirm the paper exists. The Springer link (result 1 and 2) shows the exact paper with proper citation metadata. Result 4 also confirms this same 1986 publication. Now let me check the specific author name format in the official source.

The official Springer page (results 1 and 2) displays the citation as: 
"Heckman, J.J., Robb, R. (1986)"


The cited reference lists the second author as "Richard Robb Jr" but the actual published citation and Springer page list the author as "Robb, R." without the "Jr" suffix. This is a minor author name formatting difference. The automated checker noted this mismatch ("Richard Robb Jr" vs "Richard Robb"), but this is a typical variation in how author names are formatted in citations—the initials vs. full first name, and the inclusion or omission of suffixes like "Jr" are standard formatting choices that don't indicate hallucination.

The exact title, venue, year, and DOI all match perfectly between the cited reference and the official Springer page.

EXPLANATION: The paper exists and matches on title, venue, and year; the author name variation (Richard Robb Jr vs. Richard Robb) is a minor formatting difference in how the surname suffix is presented, which is not evidence of hallucination.

FOUND_TITLE: Alternative Methods for Solving the Problem of Selection Bias in Evaluating the Impact of Treatments on Outcomes
